findings.message: `init_tracing_subscriber()` still calls `tracing_subscriber::fmt().init()`, which panics if any subscriber has already been installed elsewhere in the process. `OnceLock` only makes repeated calls through this function safe; it does not make initialization globally idempotent. In a library/crate context this is a process-level crash hazard, so this should use a non-panicking path such as `try_init()` and ignore the already-set case.

The new telemetry wiring decision point (`resolve_telemetry_writer`) has no direct test coverage in this scope. The existing tests only exercise the emit helpers and the subscriber guard, so regressions in the main branch/kill-switch gating (`track/*` vs non-track, `SOTP_TELEMETRY=0`) would not be caught.

These `temp_env` mutations run without `crate::test_support::process_env_lock()`, unlike the other env-sensitive tests in this crate. Because Rust tests execute in parallel and process env is global, these tests can race with sibling tests and flap nondeterministically.

The non-track-branch behavior of the new composition-root wiring is not covered here. This test only exercises `usecase::track_resolution::resolve_track_id_from_branch(Some("main"))`, so a regression in `resolve_telemetry_writer`'s branch guard or writer construction would still pass the suite.

`resolve_telemetry_writer` derives the telemetry output path from the supplied `items_dir`, but it derives the branch/track id from `SystemGitRepo::discover()` using the process CWD. For `--items-dir` pointing at another worktree/repo, or when the CLI is launched outside the target repo, telemetry is either suppressed despite a valid target `track/*` branch or written under the target items dir using the CWD repo's track id. That breaks the branch-bound attribution requirement; anchor git discovery to the project root derived from `items_dir` before resolving the branch.

`resolve_telemetry_writer` accepts `items_dir`, but branch discovery is still anchored to the process CWD via `SystemGitRepo::discover()`. Track commands can pass non-default `--items-dir`/workspace roots, so running from another repository or outside the target repository can either disable telemetry for a valid track operation or write under the supplied `items_dir` using a track id from the wrong repo. This breaks the branch-bound attribution guarantee; derive the project root from `items_dir` and use `discover_from` before parsing the branch.

`init_tracing_subscriber()` still installs a global fmt subscriber with a default `info` filter whenever `RUST_LOG` is unset. Since `apps/cli` calls this during startup, dependency `INFO` logs from components such as ort/lance/onnxruntime are emitted to stderr for normal `sotp` commands, contaminating command output that previously consisted of a single verdict line. The telemetry design records JSONL under the track directory, not dependency logs on stderr; default this to `warn` or only enable the fmt layer when `RUST_LOG` is explicitly set.

`review_v2/mod.rs` now has 763 lines of production code before the test block, which exceeds the repository's 700-line maximum for a production module. The new telemetry wiring pushed this module over the cap, so it needs to be split back down before merge.

These telemetry calls are unconditional after `run_codex_review_str` / `run_claude_review_str`, so `Ok(CodexReviewOutcome::Skipped { .. })` still records an `ExternalSubprocess` event even though no reviewer subprocess was spawned. That produces phantom `codex`/`claude` executions for empty-scope reviews and violates the new telemetry contract. Gate the subprocess event on an actual reviewed outcome, not on any non-error result.

Telemetry is emitted too early here: `verdict_parse_failed` is set from any `Err`, and `subprocess_ran` then becomes true for those same failures. That means `emit_external_subprocess` (and the paired `ReviewRound`) will be written even when the review never reached a subprocess, for example on composition/build failures or an invalid round type. The same pattern is repeated in `apps/cli-composition/src/dry.rs`, so the telemetry stream can contain false subprocess attempts and zero-findings rounds that never actually ran.

Telemetry for the review round is only emitted in the `Ok(FinalCompleted|FastCompleted)` arm. If the reviewer subprocess runs and then fails later (parse/timeout/abort, or any other post-launch `Err`), both `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` are skipped, so the new `verdict_parse_failed=true` branch can never be recorded in production.

The dry-check path has the same success-only gate: `if let Ok(ref findings) = dry_result` drops telemetry on any `Err`. That means failed dry-check runs never emit `ExternalSubprocess` or the `ReviewRound` event, even though the new contract expects failure-path telemetry as well.

This telemetry block runs before `run_result?`, so any pre-spawn failure from `build_v2_shared`/`dispatch_review_cycle` (for example missing scope files, missing `.commit_hash`, or an unknown round type) is still recorded as `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess`. That logs attempts that never launched the reviewer as completed rounds.

`run_dry_check` can fail during reader/index/candidate preparation before any `CodexDryChecker::judge` subprocess is invoked, but this block still emits `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` afterward. On those failures the code also hardcodes `verdict_parse_failed=false`, so unsuccessful dry-check attempts are logged as successful subprocess runs.

`emit_external_subprocess` is always called with `verdict_parse_failed = false` here, and the same pattern is repeated for the Claude and local-review branches. Because `dispatch_review_cycle` collapses reviewer failures into `SubprocessFailed`, parse failures and launcher/setup failures are both recorded exactly like successful subprocesses, so the `verdict_parse_failed=true` telemetry path can never be produced.

This block emits `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` after every `run_dry_check` result, but `run_dry_check` can return `Err` before any `CodexDryChecker::judge` subprocess is invoked. Those failures are therefore logged as completed dry rounds with `findings_count=0` and `verdict_parse_failed=false`, which fabricates telemetry for dry-check attempts that never actually ran.

`SubprocessFailed` is produced for every `Err` from `cycle.review()` / `cycle.fast_review()`, but those errors are not all subprocess failures. `ReviewCycle` can return `FileChangedDuringReview`, diff/hash, or other pre-review failures, so this branch will emit `ExternalSubprocess` with `verdict_parse_failed=true` even when the subprocess did not fail (and may not have been the source of the error).

`run_dry_check()` errors are treated as subprocess failures here, but that service can fail before any `DryCheckAgentPort::judge()` call (reader/index/candidate preparation). As written, those pre-agent failures still emit `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` telemetry with `verdict_parse_failed=true`, which records a subprocess event that never happened.

The new `ExternalSubprocess` telemetry is only emitted inside the `Ok(FastCompleted|FinalCompleted)` branch, and it is always recorded with `verdict_parse_failed = false`. That means any reviewer subprocess that exits non-zero, fails to parse its verdict, or otherwise returns `Err` produces no subprocess telemetry at all. The new event contract explicitly has a failure flag, so this needs an error-aware emission path instead of dropping the record on failures. The same pattern is repeated in the Claude/local paths and in `dry.rs`.

`load_agent_profiles_from_repo()` still discovers the repository from the process cwd via `SystemGitRepo::discover()`, even though `review_run_local` and `review_run_fix_local` already have `items_dir` and the rest of the composition root now anchors repo-aware operations off that path. As written, a launcher or wrapper that `cd`s elsewhere can make `sotp review local` / `fix-local` fail to load `agent-profiles.json` even when the target repo is valid and discoverable from `items_dir`. This helper should anchor profile loading to the resolved repo root, not the cwd.

This telemetry gate only emits for `Ok(FastCompleted|FinalCompleted)`. Any reviewer failure (`ReviewCycleError::Reviewer` for timeout/abort/illegal output, or other post-launch errors) drops both `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess`, so the new failure-path record (`verdict_parse_failed=true`) can never be produced. The same pattern is repeated in `review_run_claude` and `review_run_local`.

The dry-check telemetry is gated on `Ok(findings)` as well, so any `DryCheckCycleError` that happens after `DryCheckAgentPort::judge()` (agent failure, entry construction/write failure, etc.) produces no `ReviewRound` or `ExternalSubprocess` event. That leaves the new dry-round failure telemetry path unrecorded.

A `write_verdict` / `write_fast_verdict` failure returns `Err(...)` before the telemetry block runs, so a review that already launched its subprocess will not emit `ReviewRound` or `ExternalSubprocess` when persistence fails. The new instrumentation contract says launched subprocesses must still be recorded even on later I/O failure.

This sets `verdict_parse_failed=true` for every `SubprocessFailed`, but the field means the subprocess stdout verdict could not be parsed. `ReviewerError` includes timeout, abort, and unexpected failures, and the dry-check path repeats the same pattern for agent/entry/writer errors, so telemetry will report parse failures for non-parse failures.

The `SubprocessFailed` telemetry path assumes every `ReviewerError`/`DryCheckAgentError` means the subprocess already ran, but `Unexpected` is also used for pre-launch setup/spawn failures. That will write `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` records for attempts that never started a reviewer/agent process. The same pattern is repeated in the Claude/local review wrapper and in `apps/cli-composition/src/dry.rs`.

`write_verdict`/`write_fast_verdict` failures are converted into `SubprocessFailed`, but the telemetry mapper later hardcodes `findings_count = 0` for every `SubprocessFailed`. If the review subprocess already produced a verdict and then persistence failed, the emitted `ReviewRound` will underreport the actual findings.

This telemetry gate treats `ReviewerError::Unexpected` as if it only covered pre-launch setup, but the reviewer adapters also return `Unexpected` after the subprocess has already run (for example when polling/reaping the child, reading `--output-last-message`, or re-rendering the verdict fails). In those cases `run_result` becomes `Err`, so launched reviews now skip both `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` emission. The same pattern is repeated in the `review_run_claude` and `review_run_local` blocks below.

`DryCheckAgentError::Unexpected` is not just a pre-launch error here: the Codex dry-check adapter also returns it after the subprocess has run when polling, waiting, or reading `--output-last-message` fails. This branch therefore drops real subprocess failures from telemetry, so launched dry-check runs can complete with no `ReviewRound`/`ExternalSubprocess` event at all.

`Unexpected` is treated as a pre-launch failure here, so it bypasses `SubprocessFailed` and the telemetry hooks in `mod.rs`. That is not safe: the reviewer/dry-check infrastructure also uses `Unexpected` for post-spawn failures such as child polling, reaping, and output-read errors, so launched subprocess failures can now disappear from `ReviewRound` / `ExternalSubprocess` telemetry. The same mistake is repeated in `apps/cli-composition/src/dry.rs`.

`ReviewerError::Unexpected` is still classified as `SubprocessFailed`, but that error can be raised before Codex/Claude is spawned (path prep, schema write, spawn failure). As a result, `review_run_codex`/`review_run_claude` will emit `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` telemetry for runs where no subprocess actually executed.

`DryCheckAgentError::Unexpected` is treated as a subprocess-involved failure, but `CodexDryChecker::run_agent` can return it before spawn (runtime-path prep, file writes, `spawn_codex`). That makes the dry-check wrapper emit `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` telemetry even when no external process ran.

`ReviewerError::Unexpected` is used for both pre-spawn setup failures (`spawn_codex` / `spawn_claude`) and post-launch failures, but this arm converts every one of them into `SubprocessFailed`. That makes `review_run_codex`, `review_run_claude`, and `review_run_local` emit `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` telemetry for attempts that never launched a reviewer, with `findings_count` forced to 0.

`DryCheckAgentError::Unexpected` covers pre-spawn setup failures (`prepare_runtime_path`, file initialization) as well as post-spawn errors, but this branch treats every one of them as a launched subprocess. As written, `dry_write` will record `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` telemetry for dry-check attempts that failed before `spawn_codex`, again with zero findings.

`build_v2_shared()` still uses `SystemGitRepo::discover()` from the process CWD. The new `load_agent_profiles_from_repo(Some(items_dir))` path makes local review setup look items-dir anchored, but composition setup can still fail or bind the wrong repository when the CLI is launched from outside the repo root with a valid `--items-dir`.

`dispatch_review_cycle()` records `findings_count: 0` for `ReviewCycleError::FileChangedDuringReview`, even though `cycle.review()` / `fast_review()` already produced a verdict before the hash-after mismatch. That makes the new telemetry count wrong on a main post-subprocess failure path. The fast-review branch mirrors the same issue.

The telemetry match only emits `ReviewRound` / `ExternalSubprocess` for `FastCompleted`, `FinalCompleted`, and `SubprocessFailed`. `CodexReviewOutcome::Skipped` is dropped in all three review entry points, so empty-scope review invocations never appear in telemetry even though they are valid review rounds with zero findings. That creates a silent observability gap for the no-diff path.

Post-subprocess failures are still dropped from telemetry here: `review_telemetry_for_outcome()` returns `None` on `Err`, so a `Diff`/`Hash` error from the second hash check or a verdict-rendering failure after the reviewer has already run will skip both `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess`. That violates the new instrumentation contract for subprocess-launched failures.

This match treats every `DryCheckCycleError::Index` as pre-subprocess, but the dry-check interactor can raise `Index` after `judge()` during per-candidate `changed_path` validation. Those rounds have already launched the agent, so returning `None` here suppresses the required telemetry for a real subprocess-involved failure.

`dry_subprocess_telemetry_for_result()` collapses every post-spawn failure to `findings_count = 0` unless the entire `run_dry_check()` succeeds. That loses counts for rounds that already recorded one or more findings before an `Agent`/`Entry`/`Writer`/`Index` error, so the new `ReviewRound.findings_count` field can be materially wrong on failure paths.

`round_start` is captured before `run_codex_review_str()` / `run_claude_review_str()`, then reused for `emit_external_subprocess()`. The event contract says the timer should start immediately before subprocess launch, so the emitted `duration_ms` includes composition work, repo discovery, and other pre-spawn overhead rather than just the subprocess runtime. The same pattern is duplicated on the dry path.

The refactor introduced three public review entrypoints here (`review_run_codex`, `review_run_claude`, `review_run_local`), but this scope only has a single branch-error regression for `review_run_codex`. There is no happy-path coverage for codex and no direct unit coverage at all for claude/local, so regressions in provider resolution, telemetry wiring, or verdict shaping can slip through. That violates the repo rule requiring both happy-path and error-path tests for every public API.

`dry_agent_error_is_subprocess_failure()` classifies several `DryCheckAgentError::Unexpected(...)` messages as post-spawn failures, but the matching errors are raised in `CodexDryChecker::build_prompt()` before `run_agent()` launches the Codex subprocess. That makes `dry_write()` emit `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` telemetry for failures that never started a child process, which breaks the event contract.

P1: The dry-round telemetry count is reconstructed from already-persisted records only. On `Entry`/`Writer`/post-judge `Index` failures, the current `Violation` judgment has not been appended yet, so `count_new_violation_records()` underreports `ReviewRound.findings_count` for failure paths. Carry the in-flight judgment/result count from the agent wrapper instead of deriving it only from `dry-check.json`.

`with_repo_cwd` returns `Err` when restoring the previous cwd fails, even if the review closure already succeeded. In `review_run_codex`/`review_run_claude`/`review_run_local`, that turns a successfully written review into a command failure and also skips the new telemetry emission because callers only emit on `Ok` outcomes. A restore failure should not discard the primary result.

`resolve_telemetry_writer` builds `TelemetryWriter` from the raw `items_dir` path. Because the review code elsewhere canonicalizes the repo root and CLI `items_dir` is often the relative default `track/items`, running from a subdirectory can write telemetry under the wrong tree instead of the actual track directory. This makes the new ReviewRound/ExternalSubprocess events land in the wrong place or split across directories.

P1: `validate_all_paths` does not actually reject all Windows drive-letter paths. The current check only filters `C:\` / `C:/`, so inputs like `C:foo` or `C:` still pass validation even though the docstring says drive-letter prefixes are disallowed. That lets `review classify` accept non-repo-relative paths on Windows.

`dry_subprocess_telemetry_for_result()` treats every `Ok(findings)` as evidence that a dry-check subprocess ran, so `dry_write()` will emit `ExternalSubprocess` even on success paths where no candidate pair ever invoked `DryCheckAgentPort::judge()` (for example, an empty diff or zero above-threshold candidates). In those cases `agent_recorder.started_at()` is `None`, but the code still writes a subprocess event using `dry_start`, which makes the telemetry claim a subprocess ran when none did. Gate the emit on a real agent start.

`resolve_telemetry_context_from_branch()` ignores the caller-provided `items_dir` and always rewrites telemetry to `repo.root()/track/items`. That regresses any command using a non-default repo-local `--items-dir` (the review and dry entrypoints all accept one): telemetry will be written under the default track/items path instead of alongside the actual run data, so those runs silently lose/misplace telemetry.

Telemetry is resolved after the command finishes by re-reading the current branch (`resolve_telemetry_writer(&input.items_dir)`), so a branch switch during a long review or dry run can write `ReviewRound`/`ExternalSubprocess` records under the wrong `track_id` or suppress them entirely. Reuse the track context captured at command start instead; the same issue also affects the dry-write path.

P1: `dry_write` emits telemetry through `resolve_telemetry_writer_for_track` using the explicit `--track-id`, but this path never checks that the current branch is `track/<id>` or even a `track/*` branch. With `SOTP_TELEMETRY=1`, running `sotp dry write --track-id <id>` from `main` can still append ReviewRound/ExternalSubprocess events, violating the branch-bound telemetry contract. Capture/validate branch context at command start, or use the branch-resolving telemetry path for dry-write before emitting.

AC-08/CN-08 require the telemetry report output to include the skipped-line count, but the formatter only prints that section when `skipped_lines > 0`. Clean or empty reports therefore omit the skip count entirely, so the public report does not consistently expose the required fail-open parse summary.

These filesystem operations still use `items_dir`/`project_root` as relative paths (`src_dir`, `archive_root`, `src_logs`, `dst_logs`), so if the command is invoked from anywhere other than the repo root it will look for and move the wrong paths. Only `git mv` is anchored to `repo.root()`, which makes the source check and `logs/` rename inconsistent with the discovered repository root.

P1: The new public `track_archive` flow is only covered by the happy-path test added here. There is no test for the documented failure cases or for the normal no-`logs/` path, so regressions in error propagation or the conditional `logs/` move would slip through.

`track_archive` mutates the worktree before the operation is known to have succeeded, and it never रोलs back. If `std::fs::rename(&src_logs, &dst_logs)` fails after `git mv` has already moved the tracked files, the function returns `Err` with the repository left half-archived: the source track dir is gone, the archive dir exists, and the logs remain behind. A retry cannot complete cleanly from the original source path.

The production portion of this module now extends to line 793 before the `#[cfg(test)]` block, exceeding the AGENTS.md hard limit of 700 production lines. The base version was below the limit at 683 lines, so adding `track_archive` and its rollback helper here creates a new module-size/architecture violation; move the archive implementation into a submodule such as `track::ops`/`track::archive` and keep this composition module under the cap.

If `std::fs::rename(&src_logs, &dst_logs)` fails after `git mv` has already moved the track directory, the function returns an error with the archive left half-complete. Because the command also rejects any later retry once `track/archive/<id>` exists, a transient logs-move failure becomes unrecoverable through this API and leaves the repository in an inconsistent state.

The production portion of this module now runs through line 771 before `#[cfg(test)]`, so it contains 771 non-test lines and exceeds the 700-line hard cap in AGENTS.md. This is an architecture violation; split the archive logic into a submodule instead of keeping it in `track/mod.rs`.

The new DTO tests only cover successful serialization/round-trips. The briefing explicitly asks for error-case coverage, and the later report path relies on `serde_json::from_str::<TelemetryEvent>` rejecting malformed rows, unknown `event_type` values, or rows missing required fields such as `schema_version`. Add at least one negative deserialization test so the tagged JSONL contract is guarded.

`TelemetryWriter::new` trusts the raw `track_id` string and joins it directly into `items_dir`, so a value containing path separators or `..` can escape `track/items` and write telemetry outside the intended workspace. This constructor should take a validated track-id type or reject non-slug input before building the path.

The 4096-byte guard only checks the pre-truncation serialization result, then returns `line2` without verifying that the truncated JSON still fits. A long `track_id`/`command`, or JSON escaping that expands the encoded output, can still produce an oversized row while `write()` returns `Ok(())`. Recheck the second serialization length and fail if it still exceeds the cap.

`safe_path_component` does not actually reduce `track_id` to a single path component: it keeps every normal component and rejoins them with `/`. A `track_id` like `foo/bar` will therefore write to `items_dir/foo/bar/logs/telemetry.jsonl` instead of the intended single-track directory, which can make later report/archive code miss the file.

The oversized-line fallback truncates raw bytes and appends `\n`. If the event contains multibyte UTF-8 or JSON escape sequences, this can emit invalid UTF-8/JSONL, so downstream readers that use text parsing will fail on the whole file instead of just skipping the oversized row.

`TelemetryWriter::new` silently rewrites invalid `track_id` strings by taking the last normal path segment (or `unknown`). If a path-like or otherwise invalid ID reaches this constructor, distinct logical tracks can be merged into the same `telemetry.jsonl` instead of failing fast, which is a data-corruption risk.

`SOTP_TELEMETRY_DIR` is read with `std::env::var`, so a valid non-UTF8 filesystem path is treated as absent. On Unix this silently falls back to the default track directory and makes the override knob unreliable for a real path value.

New public `RoundType::from_str` has no unit tests covering either the valid `fast`/`final` parses or the unknown-value error path, so a regression in the canonical round-type parser could ship unnoticed.

Empty/whitespace-only lines are dropped before parsing, so they are neither counted nor reported in `skipped_lines`. That violates the fail-open contract for invalid JSON lines: a blank line is malformed JSON and should be skipped and counted rather than silently ignored. The accompanying test also codifies the undercounting behavior.

`aggregate` accepts a raw `&str` and joins it directly onto `items_dir` without validating it as a `TrackId`. A caller can pass `../...` segments and make the reader walk outside `track/items/<id>`, which violates the domain validation rule and creates a path-traversal read risk. Validate with `domain::TrackId::try_new` (or take `TrackId`) before building the path.

The `exists()` pre-checks collapse filesystem errors into absence: `Path::exists()` returns `false` for inaccessible paths, so a permission/metadata failure on the track directory is misreported as `TrackNotFound`, and the same problem on `telemetry.jsonl` returns an empty report instead of `Io`. That silently swallows real I/O failures. Distinguish `NotFound` from other errors via `metadata`/`open` error handling instead of using `exists()` as the gate.

`reader.lines()` decodes each line as UTF-8 before the JSON parser sees it, and any decode failure is returned as `TelemetryReportError::Io`. That breaks the required fail-open reader behavior for invalid JSON lines: a single corrupted/non-UTF-8 JSONL line will fail the whole report instead of incrementing `skipped_lines` and continuing. Read raw lines with `read_until(b'\n')`/`serde_json::from_slice` or otherwise treat UTF-8/JSON parse failures as skipped lines.

`execute_track_with_telemetry` resolves telemetry against a hard-coded `track/items` path instead of the active command's `items_dir`/`workspace_root`. For any track subcommand run with a non-default track tree, the emitted `TrackSubcommand`/`NonZeroExit` records will be written under the wrong repository path or dropped entirely, so telemetry no longer follows the command's actual target track.

`execute_track_with_telemetry()` always resolves the telemetry writer from `cmd.items_dir()`, but `TrackCommand::items_dir()` returns the constant `track/items` for commands that are actually rooted at `project_root`/`workspace_root` (`Views::Sync`, `TypeSignals`, `BaselineCapture`, `Lint`, `CatalogueImplSignals`, etc.). If one of those commands is run with a non-default root, telemetry will be written under the wrong track tree or dropped entirely.

`NonZeroExit` is emitted with `error_chain` hard-coded to `""`, so every failing track command loses the human-readable failure chain required by the telemetry schema/brief. The current telemetry log already shows empty `error_chain` values, which makes these failure events ineffective for debugging.

Telemetry attribution is still anchored to the process CWD. `execute_track_with_telemetry()` now passes the right `items_dir`, but `resolve_telemetry_writer()` still discovers the branch from `SystemGitRepo::discover()`, so running a track command with `--project-root`/`--workspace-root` pointing at another repo or worktree can either suppress telemetry or write it under the wrong `track_id`. That breaks the branch-bound telemetry requirement.

`execute_with_error_chain()` only produces a chain for `CliError`, but the common track failure path is a non-zero `ExitCode` returned from downstream composition. Those failures still reach `emit_non_zero_exit()` with `error_chain = ""`, so the recorded `NonZeroExit` events do not carry the failure chain required by IN-03. The telemetry fixture already shows empty chains on the failing events.

`SetCommitHash` is explicitly supported from nested working directories, but this accessor returns the relative `track/items` path. `execute_track_with_telemetry` uses that value to construct the telemetry writer before dispatch, so `bin/sotp track set-commit-hash` run from a subdirectory will append telemetry under `<subdir>/track/items/<id>/logs/telemetry.jsonl` while the command itself writes `.commit_hash` under the repo root. That breaks the track-local retention/output path contract and can create stray logs outside the real track directory. Resolve this variant's telemetry items dir from the discovered repo root instead of returning a CWD-relative default.

This generated runtime telemetry log is being added under `apps/cli/track/items/.../logs/telemetry.jsonl`, which is outside the intended `track/items/<id>/logs` tree and bypasses the repository ignore rule for `track/items/**/logs/`. Committing it pollutes the source tree with local command history and contradicts the requirement that telemetry logs are not checked in. Remove the generated log and make sure telemetry writes during local/test runs are either isolated or land under the ignored track log path.

This nested ignore rule hides `apps/cli/track/`, which is exactly the non-canonical telemetry tree the previous finding called out. The canonical and already-ignored path is repo-root `track/items/<id>/logs/`; adding an app-local ignore masks future stray telemetry writes under `apps/cli` instead of preventing or removing them, leaving local command history outside the intended track retention path.

`apps/cli/.gitignore` says no local ignore rule is needed because the root `track/items/**/logs/` rule covers telemetry logs, but that root-relative pattern does not ignore nested `apps/cli/track/...` logs. `git check-ignore` returns no match and the scoped `apps/cli/track/items/.../logs/telemetry.jsonl` file is currently addable, so telemetry artifacts can be committed from this crate path, violating AC-10 and risking log pollution. Add a local ignore rule for this nested track log path or remove the generated tree.

`apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs` now has 730 production lines before the `#[cfg(test)]` section, up from 628 in HEAD, exceeding the repository's 700-line maximum for production module size. The new `TrackCommand::items_dir` implementation and dispatch refactor should be moved into narrower submodules or otherwise split so this module stays within the enforced architecture limit.

`track/` in this crate-local ignore file matches any directory named `track` below `apps/cli`, not just the stray runtime artifact path. This currently hides the new `apps/cli/src/commands/track/dispatch.rs` file (`git check-ignore` reports this rule), while `apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs` now declares `mod dispatch;`. If committed as-is without force-adding that ignored source file, a clean checkout will fail to compile with the dispatch module missing. Narrow this ignore pattern, e.g. `/track/` or `/track/items/**/logs/`, and ensure `dispatch.rs` is tracked.

`GateEval` telemetry is not carrying the evaluated result. `commands::verify::execute()` only returns an `ExitCode`, so this wrapper fabricates `reason_summary` as `gate: ...` and hashes the CLI path string / placeholder instead of the evaluated artifact. That violates the T005 contract: `reason_summary` should summarize findings and `input_hash` should identify the artifact, so branch-resolved verify variants collapse to the same hash and the event loses the actual failure context.

Telemetry writer discovery is anchored to `PathBuf::from("track/items")` from the process CWD. `verify.rs` accepts `--project-root`, `--workspace-root`, `--items-dir`, and `--track-dir`; when a user runs `sotp verify ...` from outside the repo root or against another checkout, `resolve_telemetry_writer` will look in the wrong place and silently drop or misattribute telemetry even though the command itself succeeds. The wrapper needs to derive the items dir from the command inputs or reuse the same root-resolution logic as the underlying command.

`GateEval` summaries fall back to `gate: ...` for `catalogue-spec-refs` failures because the wrapper only carries stdout, but that gate reports its findings on stderr. The emitted telemetry therefore loses the actual failure context instead of reflecting the evaluated result.

The 256-character truncation uses `t[..256]`, which can panic on valid UTF-8 stdout containing multibyte characters before byte 256. A verify result with non-ASCII text will crash the CLI instead of emitting telemetry.

The `input_hash` for `CatalogueSpecRefs` and `CatalogueSpecSignals` is derived from a canonical string rather than the resolved command inputs. Custom `--items-dir` or alternate workspace roots therefore collapse to the same hash, so telemetry cannot distinguish distinct evaluations.

`resolve_telemetry_writer()` runs before the hook outcome is inspected, so every hook invocation now does branch discovery / git filesystem work even when no telemetry will be emitted. That violates the stated allow-path contract of 'no file IO' and makes otherwise no-op hook executions pay an unnecessary I/O cost.

`VerifyCommand::items_dir()` now forwards the caller's explicit `--items-dir`/`--workspace-root` path, but `resolve_telemetry_writer()` still only resolves the canonical `<project-root>/track/items` layout. Any valid verify invocation that uses a custom items directory now returns `None` here, so `GateEval` telemetry is silently skipped for those runs.

For `CatalogueSpecRefs`, the branch-resolved case hardcodes `track/items/{track_id}` instead of reusing the actual `items_dir` passed to the verifier. With an absolute or non-default `--items-dir`, the emitted `input_hash` no longer identifies the same artifact path that `verify_catalogue_spec_refs` evaluated.

Fail-closed hook errors return exit code 2 in the `Err(msg)` branch, but that branch never calls `emit_hook_block`. Internal hook failures are therefore invisible in telemetry even though they satisfy the block verdict.

`verify_command_input_hash` hashes `Path::to_string_lossy()` output, so non-UTF-8 inputs are normalized before hashing. That violates the documented "SHA-256 of the path bytes" contract and can make distinct paths collide or produce different digests than the actual input bytes.

`CatalogueSpecSignals` hashes `workspace_root` here, but the gate actually evaluates the contents under `--items-dir`. Two invocations against different items roots in the same workspace will therefore emit the same `input_hash`, so the GateEval record no longer identifies the primary evaluated artifact.

The branch-resolved fallback collapses every path-less verify variant to `track/items/{track_id}`. That is not the resolved primary path for `SpecStates`, which chooses `track/items/<track_id>/spec.md` when `--spec-path` is omitted, so `verify spec-states` telemetry loses the file identity and collides with other branch-resolved gates.

`GateEval` telemetry still loses the real failure context on composition errors. `run_capturing()` returns `None` for `Err(msg)`, so `execute_verify_with_telemetry()` falls back to `"gate: {gate_name}"` instead of the actual error text. That makes `reason_summary` misleading on the failure path and violates the brief’s requirement that it reflect the real output/findings.

`input_hash` is computed from `String::from_utf8_lossy(...)` for every path-based verify variant. On Unix, any non-UTF-8 path bytes get replaced before hashing, so the digest is not over the real path bytes and can collide for distinct legal paths. Hash the raw path encoding instead of a lossy UTF-8 string.

`VerifyCommand::items_dir()` returns a fixed relative `track/items` for the path-based variants (`SpecAttribution`, `SpecFrontmatter`, `SpecSignals`, `SpecStates`, `PlanArtifactRefs`). When those subcommands are invoked with absolute `--spec-path`/`--track-dir` values from outside the repo root, `execute_verify_with_telemetry()` resolves the writer against the wrong base directory and silently drops `GateEval` for a valid run.

The new CLI telemetry wrappers introduce several branching paths (`GateEval`, `HookBlock`, `AdvisoryHookFired`, success/error forwarding), but there are no tests covering the new entry points or the new success/failure branches. That leaves the telemetry emission logic and stdout/stderr forwarding unguarded against regressions.

Branch-resolved verify runs no longer hash the artifact alone: when `verify_command_input_hash()` falls back to the synthetic sentinel, `execute_verify_with_telemetry()` appends `:{gate_name}` before hashing. That makes `input_hash` differ across verify subcommands even when they evaluate the same underlying artifact, which breaks the contract that the field identify the artifact/path.

The new telemetry branches are effectively untested. The added tests only smoke-check `tech-stack`, `layers`, and the advisory hook allow path; none assert `GateEval`/`HookBlock`/`AdvisoryHookFired` emission or the spec/catalogue/branch-resolved verify paths. A regression in the new match arms or hash routing would ship without coverage.

`CatalogueSpecRefs` without an explicit `--track-id` hashes a hardcoded `track/items/<track_id>` path here instead of the command's actual `items_dir`. That makes `input_hash` wrong for non-default `--items-dir` invocations, and it also bakes in a Unix separator instead of deriving the joined path from `a.items_dir()`.

For branch-resolved verify commands, `cmd.items_dir()` still falls back to the raw relative `track/items` path when the path arg is omitted (`SpecStates(None)` and `PlanArtifactRefs(None)`). Those commands resolve their real inputs from the discovered git repo root, so running `sotp verify spec-states` or `sotp verify plan-artifact-refs` from a repo subdirectory will make `resolve_telemetry_writer()` point at the wrong tree and silently skip or misplace `GateEval` telemetry even though the gate itself succeeds. Anchor the telemetry root to the resolved repo/project root instead of the CWD-relative fallback.

The branch-resolved `SpecStates` and `PlanArtifactRefs` cases fall back to `PathBuf::from(".")`, but `resolve_telemetry_writer()` only recognizes a `<project-root>/track/items` layout. As written, `execute_verify_with_telemetry()` will never emit `GateEval` for the no-argument `verify spec-states` / `verify plan-artifact-refs` paths, so two of the 20 verify variants are silently uninstrumented.

For `CatalogueSpecRefs`, the branch-resolved path hashes only `items_dir` when `track_id` is omitted, even though the actual evaluated artifact is `items_dir/<track_id>`. On a `track/<id>` branch this collapses all catalogue-spec-ref runs onto the same `input_hash`, so the emitted `GateEval` no longer identifies the artifact being checked.

`CatalogueSpecRefs` and `CatalogueSpecSignals` derive telemetry from `items_dir` alone, but both commands also accept `--workspace-root` and use it to resolve the actual repo/branch being evaluated. As written, `execute_verify_with_telemetry()` will still resolve the writer and build `input_hash` from the current CWD's `track/items` tree for a valid cross-worktree invocation, so GateEval gets misattributed or silently dropped for the real repository.

`CatalogueSpecRefs` and `CatalogueSpecSignals` still hash the raw `items_dir` field here instead of the resolved `VerifyCommand::items_dir()`. When `--workspace-root` is non-default, the telemetry writer is anchored to the correct repository but `input_hash` is still derived from the current CWD's `track/items/...`, so cross-worktree/CI runs get the wrong artifact hash.

The branch-resolved sentinels are plain ASCII strings, so a legitimate explicit `--spec-path` or `--track-dir` equal to `track-branch-resolved:spec` or `track-branch-resolved:track-dir` will be rewritten here before hashing. That changes `input_hash` for a legal input path instead of hashing the path the user actually supplied.

`items_dir()` for `SpecAttribution`, `SpecFrontmatter`, and `SpecSignals` only resolves telemetry when the supplied `spec_path` already sits under `track/items/...`. Valid invocations with a bare or differently rooted spec path (for example `spec.md` from the track directory) fall back to `.` here, `resolve_telemetry_writer()` returns `None`, and `execute_verify_with_telemetry()` silently skips `GateEval` on a track branch.

Relative `--items-dir` values are rewritten to `<workspace_root>/track/items` whenever `workspace_root != "."`. That changes the telemetry anchor for legitimate non-default relative paths, so `catalogue-spec-refs` / `catalogue-spec-signals` can record telemetry under the wrong repository instead of the path actually used by the verifier.

The branch-resolved hash cases use synthetic relative strings (`track/items/<track_id>/spec.md` and `track/items/<track_id>`) rather than the actual resolved filesystem path that the command evaluates. The same gate invoked from a different working directory will therefore produce a different `input_hash` for the same artifact, so the telemetry no longer identifies the real input bytes.

GateEval.reason_summary is derived from the raw verify output/error text, so failed runs can persist unredacted filesystem paths and other internal details into telemetry JSONL logs. Sanitize or redact the summary before emitting it.

The CatalogueSpecRefs/CatalogueSpecSignals input hash only fingerprints items_dir (plus track_id for refs), so distinct evaluations that differ by workspace_root or skip_stale collapse to the same GateEval.input_hash. That makes the new telemetry ambiguous for different workspaces using the same track tree.

`CatalogueSpecSignals` now switches telemetry anchoring to `workspace_root`, but this command is still CWD-anchored for branch gating. When `--workspace-root` points at a different checkout/worktree, `execute_verify_with_telemetry()` can resolve the writer and `input_hash` against a different repo/branch than the command actually used, so `GateEval` can be misattributed or emitted for a skipped run.

The spec-path variants derive the telemetry root from `spec_path.parent().parent()`. That only works for canonical `track/items/<id>/spec.md`; a valid explicit path outside that tree (for example `/tmp/spec.md`) resolves to a non-`track/items` directory, so `resolve_telemetry_writer()` returns `None` and `GateEval` is silently dropped even though the verify command ran.

`CatalogueSpecRefs` computes two different `input_hash` values for the same resolved artifact depending on whether `--track-id` was passed. In the explicit case the `workspace_root` suffix is appended after the track id, but in the implicit branch-resolved case it is embedded before the resolved id when the sentinel is expanded. On non-default `--workspace-root` runs that breaks hash stability and makes identical evaluations look different to telemetry consumers.

`VerifyCommand::items_dir()` returns raw relative paths for the branch-resolved verify paths (`SpecStates(None)`, `PlanArtifactRefs(None)`, and `CatalogueSpecSignals`). `execute_verify_with_telemetry()` then passes that path verbatim into `resolve_telemetry_writer()`, so when the CLI is launched from a subdirectory or with a non-default `--workspace-root`, the gate itself resolves the repo root correctly but telemetry is written under the wrong `track/items` tree or dropped entirely.

`reason_summary` is always truncated to 256 bytes before emitting `GateEval`, even though the accepted contract says the D3 4096-byte JSONL line cap defines this field's length budget and the telemetry writer already enforces that cap. For gates with longer failure output, the event will drop most of the human-readable diagnostics instead of recording the agreed gate-output summary.

The new top-level `Telemetry` route is not covered by an end-to-end test that exercises `Cli::try_parse_from(["sotp", "telemetry", ...])` and `run_cli`, so a regression in the public CLI registration or dispatch path would ship unnoticed. The tests in `telemetry.rs` only cover a synthetic parser, not the actual binary entrypoint.

`track archive` is now routed through the generic telemetry path, but that path resolves the writer from the pre-archive `items_dir` and emits after dispatch. Since the archive command moves `track/items/<id>` into `track/archive/<id>` before `emit_track_subcommand`, the telemetry write will recreate `track/items/<id>/logs/telemetry.jsonl` instead of landing with the archived track, so the retention behavior this change is meant to add is broken for the archive command itself.

`matches!(cmd, ...)` moves `cmd` here, so the later `commands::track::execute_with_error_chain(cmd)` is a use-after-move compile error. Match on `&cmd` (or clone first) so the command can still be dispatched.

`emit_archived_track_subcommand` derives the archive log path from `items_dir.parent()` without re-anchoring to the repository root. When `sotp track archive` is run from a subdirectory with the default relative `track/items`, telemetry will be written under the current working directory instead of `<repo-root>/track/archive/<id>/logs`, diverging from the actual archive location.

This says telemetry on `main` is intentionally not recorded, but [track/workflow.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/workflow.md#L218-L219) still defines a `main`-branch fallback that resolves the latest track. As written, telemetry attribution would disagree with the current track-resolution model on the branch where ad hoc commands are commonly run.

The lock-free append plan leans on a 4096-byte `PIPE_BUF` cap, but `PIPE_BUF` is defined for pipes/FIFOs, not regular files; see https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/pipe.7.html and https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man2/write.2.html. For `telemetry.jsonl`, that does not guarantee whole-record atomic writes, so the no-lock strategy is under-specified.

The 4 KiB cap is justified with `PIPE_BUF`, but `PIPE_BUF` only constrains atomic writes to pipes/FIFOs, not regular files like `telemetry.jsonl`. As written, the no-lock append strategy does not guarantee whole-record atomicity for the target file.

This says `main`-branch executions are not recorded, but `track/workflow.md` still defines a `main` fallback that resolves the latest track. Telemetry attribution would therefore disagree with the current track-resolution model on the branch where ad hoc commands are commonly run.

AC-01 is internally inconsistent with the scope. IN-01/OS-02 and the ADR say the domain layer is not instrumented, but AC-01 only bans subscriber initialization and observation code other than tracing macros in `domain / usecase`, which would allow domain-level tracing spans/events to pass the acceptance gate. Tighten this criterion so domain-layer telemetry is forbidden outright and any allowed tracing macros are limited to the intended non-domain boundaries.

T002 introduces a new `RoundType` in `telemetry::mod`, but the telemetry contract already says `ReviewRound.round_type` uses the shared infrastructure `RoundType` enum. This splits one semantic type into two definitions and will force unnecessary conversions or duplicate serde behavior.

S2 says T002 and T003 are independently implementable/testable before composition-root wiring, but T003 explicitly depends on T002 (`前提: T002`) and its constructor/tests require `TelemetryEvent`/`TelemetryWriteError`. The dependency ordering claim is therefore false.

T003 claims CN-05 will be satisfied by using `write_all` as a single append write, but `write_all` is a retrying convenience API and is not a guarantee of one write syscall. Since the design relies on one O_APPEND write per <=4096-byte JSONL record, following this plan can violate the plan's own atomicity/record-boundary constraint.

T008 says unknown `schema_version` rows are skipped when `serde_json::from_str::<TelemetryEvent>` fails, but T002 defines `schema_version` as a plain `u32` field with derived deserialization. A future/unknown numeric version will deserialize successfully unless the plan adds explicit version validation, so AC-08/CN-08 is not actually implemented by the described algorithm.

`ReviewRound.round_type` is typed as `RoundType`, and the docs/task text describe an infrastructure `RoundType` enum with `Fast`/`Final`, but the infrastructure catalogue does not declare any `RoundType` type. That leaves implementers with a referenced type that is absent from the catalogue contract.

T004 requires emitting TrackSubcommand at both command start and completion, but the TelemetryEvent schema only has completed-command fields: exit_code and duration_ms, with no phase/kind field and no nullable values. A start event cannot populate those fields truthfully, and emitting two indistinguishable TrackSubcommand rows would also make the report's duration aggregation ambiguous. Align the task with AC-02's post-execution event, or add an explicit lifecycle field/schema for start vs finish.

T003 fixes the default output path to items_dir/track_id/logs/telemetry.jsonl and mandates opening it with OpenOptions::append(true).create(true).open(path), but no task creates the logs parent directory. create(true) creates only the file, not missing directories, so the first default-path write for a normal track dir will fail and then be suppressed by the fire-and-forget caller, breaking AC-02/AC-05. Add parent directory creation to the writer task before opening the file.

T004 tells implementers to emit `TelemetryEvent::TrackSubcommand` after every CLI dispatch once a track branch is resolved, but the spec explicitly excludes pure display commands and AC-06 requires eventless paths such as pure display commands to avoid opening `logs/`. Since task coverage maps AC-06 only to T003's lazy writer behavior, no task actually prevents T004 from recording those display commands, so following the plan can violate OS-04/AC-06.

T008 requires the report reader to extract `schema_version` from each raw JSON line before typed deserialization, but the catalogue defines `schema_version` only inside each `TelemetryEvent` variant payload and T002 asks for a derived serde struct-payload enum. With serde's default enum representation, the raw line is variant-wrapped, so a top-level version check will not find `schema_version` and valid events can be skipped or become unreadable. The plan needs an explicit wire format, such as top-level `schema_version` plus event type, or a reader that intentionally inspects the nested variant payload.

`TelemetryWriteError` is documented as errors that `TelemetryWriter` suppresses rather than propagates, but T003 defines `TelemetryWriter::write` as returning `Result<(), TelemetryWriteError>`, says IO/serialize errors are returned and suppressed by the caller, and requires a test that observes `TelemetryWriteError::Io`. These are incompatible contracts; following the catalogue would make the T003 API/tests impossible to satisfy.

T008 makes the schema-version compatibility check optional even though CN-08/AC-08 require lines with unknown `schema_version` to be skipped and counted. With the planned externally tagged `TelemetryEvent`, a known variant carrying a future `schema_version` can deserialize successfully, so an implementation may aggregate incompatible future rows instead of skipping them. Make the payload `schema_version` check mandatory before aggregation.

The writer plan requires a single `std::io::Write::write` call but does not require checking the returned byte count. `write` may return `Ok(n)` with `n < buffer.len()`, which would leave a truncated JSONL row while `TelemetryWriter::write` could still return success; this undermines the valid-JSONL and line-boundary guarantees. The task should require treating short writes as `TelemetryWriteError::Io` or otherwise explicitly handling them.

CN-03 is covered only by T001, but CN-03 includes the archive-retention requirement that gitignored `logs/` move with archived tracks; T001 only adds the `.gitignore` entry and T010 only rechecks that entry. The plan can therefore satisfy its mapped tasks while leaving GO-03's active-to-archive retention behavior unimplemented or unverified.

The plan records gate evaluations without any duration field, while the ADR goal explicitly includes finding which gates took time. T008 then aggregates phase durations only from `TrackSubcommand` events, so individual slow gates cannot be reconstructed from the planned telemetry.

CN-03/GO-03 require telemetry logs to follow the track lifecycle by moving gitignored `logs/` during archive, and task coverage maps CN-03 to T001/T010. However T010 explicitly excludes the archive-tool change as a separate future track and only verifies the `.gitignore` entry, so completing the plan can leave the stated retention/archive requirement unmet while the artifacts claim CN-03 is covered.

T001 narrows CN-03 to only adding the gitignore entry and explicitly defers archive-tool handling to a separate track, but the spec/ADR make archive movement part of the same retention requirement: GO-03 says retention follows the active to archive lifecycle, and CN-03 says the archive tool must move gitignored `logs/` with the track directory. Since `task-coverage.json` maps CN-03 only to T001 and T010 only verifies AC-10, this plan can complete while leaving the declared CN-03/GO-03 behavior unimplemented.

T008 still specifies `TelemetryReport` as if `TelemetryEvent` were externally-tagged and `schema_version` lived inside the variant payload. That contradicts the wire-format already being used for T002 in this track (internally-tagged with top-level `schema_version`), so the report adapter would be specified against the wrong JSON shape and would skip valid rows.

T005 still instructs gate evaluation emitters to include `input_hash`, but the updated ADR/spec/DTO explicitly removed that field and say it must not be recorded. This makes the implementation plan conflict with the accepted requirement set. The same stale requirement also appears in `plan.md` for T005.

GateEval's payload description is stale: it still defines `reason_summary` as a shortened list of findings, but the amended ADR/spec now define it as a truncated human-readable gate-output summary. The same block also omits the required `track_id` field that `infrastructure-types.json` still includes for `GateEval`, so the plan is describing the wrong JSONL shape.

`observations.md` records T010 as fully verified and `cargo make ci` as passed, but `plan.md` says T010 should only run after T001–T009 and T011 are complete, while `impl-plan.json` still has T011 in `todo`. That makes the recorded review state internally inconsistent and the dependency order impossible as written.

`knowledge/adr/README.md` still has unresolved merge stages in the index (`git status` reports `UU` and `git ls-files -u` lists stages 1/2/3). The worktree text contains both ADR rows in the intended order, but the conflict is not actually resolved for the merge, so the merge-preparation artifacts cannot be committed until this file is staged as resolved.

The README conflict resolution has not actually been recorded in the index: `git ls-files -u -- knowledge/adr/README.md` still shows stages 1/2/3 and `git diff --cached` reports `Unmerged path knowledge/adr/README.md`. Even though the working tree contains both ADR rows, the merge commit cannot be completed until this path is staged as resolved.
